title: Charitable Organization Donation Card Validation Testing Pattern
narrative: Fraudsters exploit nonprofit merchant accounts (MCC 8398, 8641) for card validation, betting on delayed fraud detection due to donor privacy policies and high legitimate transaction variance. Attack begins with 8-15 donations of $1.00-$3.00 spread across 12-48 hours to religious charities, disaster relief funds, or animal welfare organizations. Valid cards then receive secondary charges of $47-$89 at high-risk MCCs (5816 digital games, 5999 misc retail) within 6-18 hours. The charity donations originate from residential IPs with clean email domains (gmail, yahoo) but share identical card1_addr1 mismatches and sequential card numbers differing by 1-3 digits. Charities rarely challenge micro-donations, creating a low-friction validation environment before the bust-out phase at digital goods merchants.
indicators: card1 shows 8+ transactions under $5.00 at MCC 8398/8641 within 48-hour window
TransactionAmt pattern: initial cluster of $1-3 donations followed by $47-89 charges at MCC 5816/5999 within 18 hours
card1_txn_count_24hr jumps from 8-12 (charity phase) to 15-23 (exploitation phase) within same day
addr1 field constant across donation transactions but dist1 varies 150-800+ km between charity and secondary merchants
Sequential card1 values (last 4 digits differ by 1-3) sharing identical P_emaildomain and txn_hour clustering (22:00-04:00 UTC)
card1_amt_zscore spikes from -0.8 to +2.4 within 12-hour period as transaction amounts shift from micro to mid-range
ProductCD switches from 'C' (charity/services) to 'W' (digital goods) with no intervening legitimate purchase history
summary: Multiple small donations ($1-3) to charities within 48 hours, followed by larger charges ($47-89) at digital goods merchants within 18 hours, using sequential card numbers with mismatched billing addresses.
reasoning: Fraudsters use low-scrutiny charity donations to validate stolen cards before quickly switching to high-value digital goods purchases, exploiting nonprofits' reluctance to challenge small donor transactions and privacy policies that delay fraud detection.
indicator_explanations: Card is being validated with multiple tiny charity donations in quick succession
Amounts jump from test donations to larger fraudulent purchases once card validates
Transaction volume explodes from donation testing phase to full exploitation phase
Billing address stays fixed while purchases span impossible geographic distances
Sequential card numbers hit same charities using identical email and late-night timing
Spending pattern shifts dramatically from micro-donations to unusually large purchases
Purchase type pivots from charitable services to digital goods with no organic progression
